title: GDPR Encryption at Rest Audit Evidence

compliance.assurance_rationale: Auto-migrated from cat-22 markdown on Phase 1.3 run. Requires SME review to confirm clause accuracy, mode (satisfies vs detects-violation-of), and assurance level (contributing/partial/full).

dataSources: `aws:cloudtrail` (CreateVolume, CreateDBInstance, PutBucketEncryption), Azure Activity / resource graph exports (`mscs:azure:auditlog`), `tenable:vuln` or agent inventory with encryption flags, `gdpr_data_store_register.csv`
app: Splunk Add-on for AWS (Splunkbase 1876), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Cloud Services (Splunkbase 3110), Tenable Add-On for Splunk (Splunkbase 4060)
spl:
| inputlookup gdpr_data_store_register.csv WHERE processes_personal_data="true"
| lookup cloud_asset_encryption_lookup resource_id OUTPUT encryption_at_rest, last_scan_date, kms_key_arn
| eval scan_age_days=if(isnotnull(last_scan_date), round((now()-strptime(last_scan_date,"%Y-%m-%d"))/86400,0), 9999)
| eval compliance_status=case(
    encryption_at_rest="true", "COMPLIANT",
    encryption_at_rest="false", "NON_COMPLIANT",
    isnull(encryption_at_rest), "UNKNOWN")
| where compliance_status!="COMPLIANT" OR scan_age_days > 90
| table resource_id, system_name, data_category, encryption_at_rest, compliance_status, scan_age_days, kms_key_arn
| sort compliance_status
cimSpl: | tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Change.All_Changes by All_Changes.action, All_Changes.object_category, All_Changes.user | sort - count
description: Article 32(1)(a) requires pseudonymisation and encryption of personal data where appropriate. This use case aggregates technical evidence that storage volumes, databases, object stores, and backup targets that process personal data are encrypted at rest — surfacing misconfigured buckets, unencrypted snapshots, and hosts lacking disk encryption so the DPO can prove the measure is operational, not aspirational.

This use case aggregates technical evidence that storage volumes, databases, object stores, and backup targets that process personal data are encrypted at rest — surfacing misconfigured buckets, unencrypted snapshots, and hosts lacking disk encryption so the DPO can prove the measure is operational, not aspirational.
grandmaExplanation: (1)(a) requires pseudonymisation and encryption of personal data where appropriate — so you can show exactly what is being watched and when something went wrong.
implementation: (1) Maintain `gdpr_data_store_register.csv` linking cloud resource IDs and on-prem volumes to systems of record for personal data; (2) ingest CloudTrail / Azure audit events that reflect encryption configuration changes; (3) join nightly Tenable or CSPM exports for at-rest encryption posture; (4) alert on `NON_COMPLIANT` or stale `UNKNOWN` assets; (5) retain dashboard exports for Art. 32 accountability evidence packs.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk Add-on for AWS (Splunkbase 1876), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Cloud Services (Splunkbase 3110), Tenable Add-On for Splunk (Splunkbase 4060).
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `aws:cloudtrail` (CreateVolume, CreateDBInstance, PutBucketEncryption), Azure Activity / resource graph exports (`mscs:azure:auditlog`), `tenable:vuln` or agent inventory with encryption flags, `gdpr_data_store_register.csv`.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Maintain `gdpr_data_store_register.csv` linking cloud resource IDs and on-prem volumes to systems of record for personal data; (2) ingest CloudTrail / Azure audit events that reflect encryption configuration changes; (3) join nightly Tenable or CSPM exports for at-rest encryption posture; (4) alert on `NON_COMPLIANT` or stale `UNKNOWN` assets; (5) retain dashboard exports for Art. 32 accountability evidence packs.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
| inputlookup gdpr_data_store_register.csv WHERE processes_personal_data="true"
| lookup cloud_asset_encryption_lookup resource_id OUTPUT encryption_at_rest, last_scan_date, kms_key_arn
| eval scan_age_days=if(isnotnull(last_scan_date), round((now()-strptime(last_scan_date,"%Y-%m-%d"))/86400,0), 9999)
| eval compliance_status=case(
    encryption_at_rest="true", "COMPLIANT",
    encryption_at_rest="false", "NON_COMPLIANT",
    isnull(encryption_at_rest), "UNKNOWN")
| where compliance_status!="COMPLIANT" OR scan_age_days > 90
| table resource_id, system_name, data_category, encryption_at_rest, compliance_status, scan_age_days, kms_key_arn
| sort compliance_status
```

Understanding this SPL

**GDPR Encryption at Rest Audit Evidence (Art. 32(1)(a))** — Article 32(1)(a) requires pseudonymisation and encryption of personal data where appropriate. This use case aggregates technical evidence that storage volumes, databases, object stores, and backup targets that process personal data are encrypted at rest — surfacing misconfigured buckets, unencrypted snapshots, and hosts lacking disk encryption so the DPO can prove the measure is operational, not aspirational.

Documented **Data sources**: `aws:cloudtrail` (CreateVolume, CreateDBInstance, PutBucketEncryption), Azure Activity / resource graph exports (`mscs:azure:auditlog`), `tenable:vuln` or agent inventory with encryption flags, `gdpr_data_store_register.csv`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Add-on for AWS (Splunkbase 1876), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Cloud Services (Splunkbase 3110), Tenable Add-On for Splunk (Splunkbase 4060). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Loads rows via `inputlookup` (KV store or CSV lookup) for enrichment or reporting.
• Enriches events using `lookup` (lookup definition + optional OUTPUT fields).
• `eval` defines or adjusts **scan_age_days** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **compliance_status** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• Filters the current rows with `where compliance_status!="COMPLIANT" OR scan_age_days > 90` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• Pipeline stage (see **GDPR Encryption at Rest Audit Evidence (Art. 32(1)(a))**): table resource_id, system_name, data_category, encryption_at_rest, compliance_status, scan_age_days, kms_key_arn
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

Optional CIM / accelerated variant (same use case, normalized fields via Common Information Model):

```spl
| tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Change.All_Changes by All_Changes.action, All_Changes.object_category, All_Changes.user | sort - count
```

Understanding this CIM / accelerated SPL

**GDPR Encryption at Rest Audit Evidence (Art. 32(1)(a))** — Article 32(1)(a) requires pseudonymisation and encryption of personal data where appropriate. This use case aggregates technical evidence that storage volumes, databases, object stores, and backup targets that process personal data are encrypted at rest — surfacing misconfigured buckets, unencrypted snapshots, and hosts lacking disk encryption so the DPO can prove the measure is operational, not aspirational.

Documented **Data sources**: `aws:cloudtrail` (CreateVolume, CreateDBInstance, PutBucketEncryption), Azure Activity / resource graph exports (`mscs:azure:auditlog`), `tenable:vuln` or agent inventory with encryption flags, `gdpr_data_store_register.csv`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Add-on for AWS (Splunkbase 1876), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Cloud Services (Splunkbase 3110), Tenable Add-On for Splunk (Splunkbase 4060). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

This **CIM or accelerated** block uses normalized field names and/or `tstats` over data models. Enable **acceleration** on the referenced models (and correct CIM knowledge objects) or the search may return nothing.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Uses `tstats` against accelerated summaries for data model `Change.All_Changes` — enable acceleration for that model.
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

Enable Data Model Acceleration (and metric indexes for `mstats`) for the models or datasets referenced above; otherwise `tstats`/`mstats` may return no results from summaries.


Step 3 — Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table (non-compliant stores), Single value (count lacking encryption), Bar chart (violations by data category), Timeline (encryption configuration changes).
visualization: Table (non-compliant stores), Single value (count lacking encryption), Bar chart (violations by data category), Timeline (encryption configuration changes).

references.title: Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Cloud Services

knownFalsePositives: Administrative tasks, scheduled jobs or platform updates can match this pattern — correlate with change management, maintenance windows and user role before raising severity.
